title: References, heap and garbage collection

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java objects remain reachable through roots and reference chains; garbage collection may reclaim unreachable storage later, while aliases and long-lived references can retain objects unintentionally.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A static cache retains every request object through a map, so bounded eviction removes the unwanted root path rather than forcing a collection. Your goal is to diagnose a retention or aliasing problem using a reachability model. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Packages, builds and tests; List, Set, Map and Queue; References, identity and equality. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java objects remain reachable through roots and reference chains; garbage collection may reclaim unreachable storage later, while aliases and long-lived references can retain objects unintentionally. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Collection timing is unspecified, finalization is not reliable cleanup, a reachable object can still be a logical leak, weak reference types have special semantics, and native resources need explicit closure. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Java cannot leak memory because garbage collection frees every object that the application no longer intends to use.

includes: Explain reachability and managed reclamation without assuming collection timing or absence of leaks.
Diagnose a retention or aliasing problem using a reachability model.
excludes: Collection timing is unspecified, finalization is not reliable cleanup, a reachable object can still be a logical leak, weak reference types have special semantics, and native resources need explicit closure.

outcomes: Diagnose a retention or aliasing problem using a reachability model.

examples.title: References, heap and garbage collection: direct language case
examples.situation: A static cache retains every request object through a map, so bounded eviction removes the unwanted root path rather than forcing a collection.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A static cache retains every request object through a map, so bounded eviction removes the unwanted root path rather than forcing a collection.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.

examples.title: References, heap and garbage collection: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Two local variables alias one mutable list; changing through either reference affects the same heap object even though both variables are later reassigned independently.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Two local variables alias one mutable list; changing through either reference affects the same heap object even though both variables are later reassigned independently.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Collection timing is unspecified, finalization is not reliable cleanup, a reachable object can still be a logical leak, weak reference types have special semantics, and native resources need explicit closure.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Java cannot leak memory because garbage collection frees every object that the application no longer intends to use.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Diagnose a retention or aliasing problem using a reachability model.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Java cannot leak memory because garbage collection frees every object that the application no longer intends to use.

analogy.example: a dispatch center that tracks reachable work orders and assigns bounded tasks while protecting shared ledgers can introduce References, heap and garbage collection, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reachability, executors, races, and virtual-thread workload choice
limitations: memory visibility, scheduling, atomicity, cancellation, and garbage-collection timing cannot be inferred from human dispatch
